title: Reading box plots
description: Practice reading information presented in box plots.
topic_path: Exploring one-variable quantitative data: Summary statistics

assessment_items.question_content:
Here's a box plot that summarizes how many eggs Maya's chickens laid last week.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find the range of the data.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] eggs
hints:
###Strategy

The range is the distance between the smallest (minimum) and the largest (maximum) data points:

$\text{range} = \text{max} -\text{min}$

So if we can find the minimum and maximum from the box plot, then we can calculate the range.

###Minimum, maximum, and range

Here's how we can find the minimum and maximum in any box plot:

[[☃ image 1]]

In our box plot, the minimum is $2$ and the maximum is $10$:

[[☃ image 2]]

So the range is $10-2=8$ eggs.

options.alt: A horizontal boxplot is plotted along a horizontal axis marked from 0 to 12, in increments of 1, labeled Number of eggs laid. A left whisker extends from 2 to 3. The box extends from 3 to 8 and is divided into 2 parts by a vertical line segment at 6. The right whisker extends from 8 to 10. All values estimated.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here's a box plot that summarizes the average monthly rainfall of several cities.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Find the range of the data.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{ cm}$
hints:
###Strategy

The range is the distance between the smallest (minimum) and the largest (maximum) data points:

$\text{range} = \text{max} -\text{min}$

So if we can find the minimum and maximum from the box plot, then we can calculate the range.

###Minimum, maximum, and range

Here's how we can find the minimum and maximum in any box plot:

[[☃ image 1]]

In our box plot, the minimum is $3.5$ and the maximum is $7.5$:

[[☃ image 2]]

So the range is $7.5-3.5=4\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt: A horizontal boxplot is plotted along a horizontal axis marked from 0 to 8, in increments of 0.5, labeled Monthly rainfall (centimeters).  A left whisker extends from 3.5 to 5.5. The box extends from 5.5 to 7 and is divided into 2 parts by a vertical line segment at 6. The right whisker extends from 7 to 7.5. All values estimated.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here's a box plot that summarizes how many eggs Corey's chickens laid last week.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] eggs
hints:
###Strategy

The interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the first quartile ($\text{Q}_1$) and the third quartile ($\text{Q}_3$) in the data:

$\text{IQR} = \text{Q}_3 -\text{Q}_1$

So if we can find the quartiles from the box plot, then we can calculate the IQR.

###Quartiles and IQR

Here's how we can find the quartiles in any box plot:

[[☃ image 1]]

In our box plot, $\text{Q}_1$ is $3$ and $\text{Q}_3$ is $8$:

[[☃ image 2]]

So the IQR is $8-3=5$ eggs.

options.alt: A horizontal boxplot is plotted along a horizontal axis marked from 0 to 12, in increments of 1, labeled Number of eggs laid. A left whisker extends from 2 to 3. The box extends from 3 to 8 and is divided into 2 parts by a vertical line segment at 6. The right whisker extends from 8 to 10. All values estimated.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here's a box plot that summarizes how many eggs Yuki's chickens laid last week.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find the median of the data.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] eggs
hints:
###Strategy

The median is the middle of a data set — approximately half of the data points are above the median, and approximately half of the data points are below the median.

How can we can find the median from the box plot?

###Locating median

Here's how we can find the median in any box plot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The line through the box represents the middle of a data set:

[[☃ image 2]]

So the median is $6$ eggs.

options.alt: A horizontal boxplot is plotted along a horizontal axis marked from 0 to 12, in increments of 1, labeled Number of eggs laid. A left whisker extends from 2 to 3. The box extends from 3 to 8 and is divided into 2 parts by a vertical line segment at 6. The right whisker extends from 8 to 10. All values estimated.

How can we can find the median from the box plot?

###Locating median

Here's how we can find the median in any box plot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The line through the box represents the middle of a data set:

[[☃ image 2]]

So the median is $6$ dollars.

options.alt: A horizontal boxplot is plotted along a horizontal axis marked from 4 to 10, in increments of 0.5, labeled Cost (dollars). A left whisker extends from 5 to 5.5. The box extends from 5.5 to 7.5 and is divided into 2 parts by a vertical line segment at 6. The right whisker extends from 7.5 to 9.5. All values estimated.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here's a box plot that summarizes the number of new trees a city is planting in each neighborhood this year.

[[☃ image 3]]

**Find the median of the data.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] trees
hints:
###Strategy

The median is the middle of a data set — approximately half of the data points are above the median, and approximately half of the data points are below the median.

How can we can find the median from the box plot?

###Locating median

Here's how we can find the median in any box plot:

[[☃ image 1]]

The line through the box represents the middle of a data set:

[[☃ image 2]]

So the median is $8$ trees.

options.alt: A horizontal boxplot is plotted along a horizontal axis marked from 4 to 26, in increments of 1, labeled Number of trees. A left whisker extends from 5 to 6. The box extends from 6 to 22 and is divided into 2 parts by a vertical line segment at 8. The right whisker extends from 22 to 25. All values estimated.
